meta.title: Responding to Partner
meta.description: The responder's role in the auction

blocks.content:
## Your Turn to Talk

Your partner has opened the bidding. They've started a conversation — now it's your turn to respond. As the responder, you have a crucial job: **tell partner about YOUR hand** so the partnership can find the best contract together.

Think of it like this. Your partner said something — maybe "I have 13-21 points and five or more hearts" (a 1♥ opening) or "I have exactly 15-17 HCP and a balanced hand" (a 1NT opening). Your response is your reply: "I have support for your hearts and 8 points" or "I have a long spade suit and a good hand."

Every response you make sends a message. The better you get at choosing the right response, the more accurately your partnership will land in the right contract.

## The Responder's Principles

Before we dive into specifics, here are the guiding principles for responding:

### 1. Support Partner's Major Suit When You Can
Finding a **major-suit fit** (8+ cards between the two hands in hearts or spades) is the top priority. Major-suit games require only 10 tricks (4♥ or 4♠), while minor-suit games require 11 tricks (5♣ or 5♦). So if partner opens a major and you have 3+ card support, that's usually your most important piece of news.

### 2. Show a New Suit When You Have One
If you can't support partner's suit, your next priority is to show a suit of your own — especially a 4+ card major that partner might be able to support.

### 3. Bid Notrump as a "Nothing Else to Say" Bid
A notrump response often means: "I have some points but no great fit and no new suit to show." It's the fallback option.

### 4. Limit Your Hand When You Can
Some bids are **limited** (they tell partner your approximate point range), and some are **unlimited** (they could be weak or strong). In general, it's helpful to limit your hand early so partner can make good decisions.

### 5. Don't Respond with Fewer than 6 Points
With 0-5 points, you usually pass. Partner opened with 13-21 points, and your combined total might still be too low for any contract. Let the opening bid stand.

## What's Ahead

Each type of opening bid gets its own response system:

| Partner Opens | You'll Learn |
|--------------|-------------|
| 1♥ or 1♠ | Raises, new suits, 1NT |
| 1♣ or 1♦ | Showing majors, raising the minor, NT bids |
| 1NT | Stayman, Jacoby Transfers, quantitative raises |
| 2♣ (strong) | The 2♦ waiting bid, positive responses |
| Weak two-bid | Raising, 2NT feature ask, new suits |

Each opening bid creates a different landscape, and your response strategy adapts accordingly. But the underlying goal never changes: **help the partnership find the right contract.**

Let's start with the most exciting scenario — partner opens a major.
